A line of severe thunderstorms moved into northwestern Arkansas during the early morning hours on May 13. These storms produced damaging wind and a tornado. Severe thunderstorms redeveloped that afternoon with several damaging wind events resulting from those storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind blew out several windows at the Huntsville Middle School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Arkansas on the 14th, triggering scattered thunderstorms. A few of the storms became severe in western and southern parts of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred on U.S. Highway 270.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Hermine passed to the west and north of Arkansas. As a result, tropical moisture was pulled up into Arkansas. A stationary front concentrated the heaviest rain across northern Arkansas. The tropical moisture lingered until it was swept out of the state by a cold front on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some streets, low water bridges, and low water crossings were flooded around Harrison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather struck the four state area in far northeast Texas, northwest Louisiana, and southwest Arkansas, as a digging upper level trough intensified over Texas. This storm system moved over a very unstable low level atmosphere causing numerous hail storms with hail as large as golfballs reported in some of the thunderstorms.

An upper level disturbance slowly approached the Mid-South during the evening of April 30th, 2010 as a cold front became stationary to the west. This pattern remained in place through the evening hours of May 2nd, 2010. South to southwest winds pumped warm moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and created a very unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the front during the early evening hours and moved east into Eastern Arkansas shortly before midnight. Additional thunderstorms occurred in association with the upper level disturbance. Due to the unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms quickly became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The severe weather evolved into an outbreak by May 1st and 2nd. Historic rainfall and flash flooding in addition to large hail and damaging winds occurred during the early morning hours of May 1st with several tornadoes occurring during the afternoon hours of May 1st to early morning hours of May 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced widespread flooding in College City. Numerous streets were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through the Mid-South during the late afternoon hours of June 8th, 2010. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the warm front. Some storms became severe and produced large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Burdette. County Road 346 was washed out as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early evening of March 10, 2010, isolated thunderstorms that developed in Northeast Oklahoma ahead of a cold front and dry line moved into far Northwest Arkansas, some of which produced large hail.
The approach of a low pressure system and cold front set off isolated severe storms across extreme northern Arkansas on the afternoon of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged a dock at a resort on Lake Norfork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Arkansas on the 14th. As the front encountered unstable air over the state, severe thunderstorms were triggered in the western and northern sections during the afternoon and evening. A few of the storms continued into the early morning hours of the 15h.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell at U.S. Highway 270 and Arkansas Highway 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved down from the north and tropical moisture from the remains of Tropical Storm Bonnie moved up from the southeast, triggering isolated severe storms and localized flash flooding on the 25th through 27th.

Thunderstorms occurred near a stationary front positioned along the Kansas-Oklahoma border into extreme southern Missouri. Some of these storms produced flash flooding across portions of northwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported approximately six inches of water flowing swiftly over the northbound lanes of College Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a weak cold front triggered thunderstorms with large amounts of lightning, along with isolated instances of severe weather and flash flooding on the 15th. The front weakened as it moved farther into the state on the 16th and 17th, but it still managed to touch off isolated severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low approached from the west on the 24th. Ahead of the low, warm, moist air from the Gulf of Mexico had invaded Arkansas. When the low pressure encountered the warmth and moisture, thunderstorms erupted and some of the storms became severe. Isolated storms redeveloped in the southern part of the state early on the morning of the 25th. A cold front swept through Arkansas on the 26th. Much drier air moved into the state behind the front.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Hermine passed to the west and north of Arkansas. As a result, tropical moisture was pulled up into Arkansas. A stationary front concentrated the heaviest rain across northern Arkansas. The tropical moisture lingered until it was swept out of the state by a cold front on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low water crossings were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dangerously unstable airmass was evident across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 1st.  A strong capping inversion was in place during much of the afternoon but as a strong upper level disturbance moved into the region from the west, this cap was removed and strong supercell thunderstorms developed across the region. These storms were prolific hail producers and a few storms also spawned tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust was measured by the ASOS in Texarkana, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into hot and humid air on the 28th and 29th, bringing isolated severe storms and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some low water crossings were flooded around Parks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Arkansas from the north on the 5th, triggering scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms became severe and produced locally heavy rain. The remnants of the front continued to touch off storms from the 6th through the 8th, although the storms were less numerous on these days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large limb fell on a vehicle, breaking the back window.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the peak heating of the afternoon across portions of southwest Arkansas.  This activity developed underneath a strong upper level low pressure system which allowed for ample lift in the atmosphere. These storms produced mainly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel and quarter sized hail was reported near Gillham, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into warm, humid air over Arkansas on the 23rd and 24th. The result was some severe storms, heavy rain, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four feet of water covered the 800 block of S. St. Louis (U.S. Highway 167). Three feet of water covered Arkansas Highway 69 near the hospital. Cars stalled in the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf of Mexico surged into Arkansas at the end of April, so conditions in the state became warm and humid. On the 30th, a strong area of low pressure aloft approached from the west, resulting in the development of thunderstorms. Wind shear, a change in wind direction and speed with height, was present. This created a favorable environment for the formation of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two teenagers were injured by lightning in an old storm shelter behind a residence in Judsonia. The victims were a female 13 years old and a male 18 years old. They were sitting on metal chairs when they were injured.
